articles.title: Speaker Spotlight: Adam Greene of RXR
articles.narrative: Adam Greene, Executive Vice President of Development at RXR, discussed the future of New York City's real estate market at Commercial Observer's Future of New York Forum. He highlighted the growing momentum for office-to-residential conversions, the sustained demand for high-quality office space, and the impact of evolving industries like artificial intelligence on new development. Greene emphasized the need for more housing, strategic reinvestment in prime assets, and the construction of next-generation offices to maintain New York's economic advantage. He noted that the 467m program is effectively incentivizing conversions in Manhattan's core, particularly in areas like Midtown South, which is transforming into a dynamic live-work-play district.

articles.title: Virtual Reality Simulator SkiPod to Open Store at 572 11th Avenue
articles.narrative: SkiPod, a virtual reality skiing and snowboarding simulator, has leased a 3,790-square-foot corner storefront at 572 11th Avenue in Manhattan's Hell's Kitchen. This transaction marks one of the mixed reality company's initial retail locations. The leasing agent noted strong demand in the submarket, driven by new development, and this deal brings the retail space at 572 11th Avenue to full occupancy.

data_points.notable_features: Corner storefront in the Hell's Kitchen neighborhood.
data_points.project_notes: The retail space is located below Oskar, a 12-story luxury rental building. The Moinian Group originally developed the building and sold a 70% stake in 2024.

articles.title: Walmart Pays $223M for SoCal Cold-Storage Facility
articles.narrative: Walmart Realty, a subsidiary of Walmart, has acquired a 507,000-square-foot cold-storage facility in Riverside, CA, for $223 million from State Street Corporation. Walmart had been leasing the property since 2019, and the acquisition solidifies its grocery and food distribution operations in the Southern California market. The transaction highlights the strong demand for existing large-format cold-storage facilities in the Inland Empire, a critical hub for the region's food supply network.

data_points.notable_features: Temperature-controlled distribution facility with 42-foot clear height ceilings, 120 trailer stalls, 98 dock-high doors, and 22,500 square feet of office space. Located less than four miles from Interstates 215, State Route 60, and State Route 91.
data_points.project_notes: Walmart had been leasing the property since 2019.

articles.market_intelligence: The Inland Empire is a premier component in Southern California’s grocery supply network. Demand for temperature-controlled logistics space continues to outpace supply. Limited speculative construction coupled with robust tenant demand is expected to positively impact net absorption, vacancy, and availability in the Inland Empire industrial market over the coming quarters.

articles.title: A Big Retail Tenant Kept Saying No. Then AI Closed the Deal.
articles.narrative: NewMark Merrill Companies is leveraging artificial intelligence to enhance its retail leasing and investment strategies, according to CEO Sandy Sigal. The firm uses AI to identify optimal tenants for its shopping centers, analyze market conditions for potential acquisitions, and craft compelling pitch decks for prospective tenants. This approach has reportedly led to successful lease agreements with major retailers who had previously declined, and guided acquisition decisions by identifying the best replacement tenants for vacant spaces. Sigal emphasizes that while AI significantly streamlines the initial stages of deal-making, human expertise remains crucial for refining AI-generated content and ensuring accuracy.

articles.title: Alaris Aerospace Inks Big Industrial Lease in Palm Beach County
articles.narrative: Alaris Aerospace Systems has signed a 129,000-square-foot industrial lease at Corporate Logistics Center in Jupiter, FL, relocating its general warehousing, distribution, and office operations from Pompano Beach. Landlords Woodmont Industrial Partners and Butters Group announced the seven-year lease, which brings the recently completed facility to approximately 50% occupancy. The transaction is notable given the sluggish industrial leasing market in Palm Beach County, which saw limited net absorption in the first quarter of this year.

data_points.notable_features: Facility features 36-foot clear ceiling heights and 48 dock-high doors, located within the Palm Beach Park of Commerce near Routes 710 and 711.

articles.market_intelligence: Palm Beach County's industrial leasing market recorded only 88,128 square feet in net absorption during the first quarter of this year. Asking rents remained steady at just under $14.20 per square foot per year.

articles.title: JBG Smith Begins Office-to-Residential Conversion Project in Metro D.C.
articles.narrative: JBG Smith and Declaration Partners have commenced the conversion of 2200 Crystal Drive, an 11-story office building in Arlington, VA, into a 195-unit multifamily community. The project involves repositioning the 315,000-square-foot office structure, originally built in 1968, into residential use. BNY is providing construction financing for the development, which is located in the National Landing neighborhood.

data_points.notable_features: Located in National Landing, a neighborhood anchored by Amazon's HQ2 office.
data_points.project_notes: Office building was renovated in 2006 prior to this conversion.

articles.title: LV Collective, Peninsula Break Ground on 922-Bed Student Housing Project in College Station, Texas
articles.narrative: A joint venture between LV Collective and Peninsula Investments has broken ground on Rambler Northgate, a 922-bed student housing project in College Station, TX, near Texas A&M University. The 24-story development will feature 342 units with bed-to-bath parity and a range of amenities including a pool, coworking spaces, and a fitness center. UMB is providing the construction financing, with completion anticipated in 2028.

data_points.notable_features: 24-story building with bed-to-bath parity; amenities include a pool, coworking space, fitness center with yoga studio, sauna, cold plunge, multi-sport simulator, and jumbotron.

articles.title: Portman to Bring 30,000 SF Food Hall to Savona Mill Redevelopment in Charlotte
articles.narrative: Portman is developing Savona Food Works, a 30,000-square-foot food hall, as the anchor for its Savona Mill adaptive reuse project in Charlotte's West End district. The food hall, led by veteran operator Robert Montwaid, will feature 25 food stalls, a full-service restaurant, coffee shop, flower market, and a rooftop bar. This dining installation is part of a larger 27-acre mixed-use development that includes creative office space and multifamily units, with additional residential phases planned.

data_points.notable_features: Located in the historic "Weave building" dating back to the 1900s; adjacent to the Stewart Creek Greenway, connecting to LYNX Light Rail and Blue Line Rail Trail.
data_points.project_notes: Part of the larger Savona Mill adaptive reuse project, which includes 200,000 square feet of creative office space and 319 apartments, with more multifamily units planned for a future phase.

articles.title: Cronheim Hotel Capital Arranges $50.4M Financing for Two Hotels on Amelia Island in Florida
articles.narrative: Cronheim Hotel Capital has arranged $50.4 million in financing for two hotels on Amelia Island, Florida. The financing includes a $29.9 million loan for the refinancing and conversion of the Ocean Coast Hotel into a Holiday Inn Resort, and a separate $20.5 million loan for the refinancing of the Amelia Hotel. Both loans were originated with a regional bank and incorporate USDA and PACE components.

data_points.notable_features: Amelia Hotel is an independent beachfront hotel. Ocean Coast Hotel is being converted to a Holiday Inn Resort.
data_points.project_notes: Loans include USDA and PACE components.

articles.title: Marcus & Millichap Brokers Sale of Restaurant in Buford, Georgia Leased to Whataburger
articles.narrative: Marcus & Millichap's Taylor McMinn Retail Group has brokered the sale of a Whataburger-occupied restaurant in Buford, GA. The property is subject to a 15-year ground lease with 10% rent increases and extension options. The transaction involved an out-of-state buyer utilizing an all-cash 1031 exchange, with the seller being a repeat client developer for Whataburger.

data_points.notable_features: Property is subject to a 15-year ground lease with 10% rent increases in the initial term and extension options.
data_points.project_notes: The buyer purchased the restaurant in an all-cash 1031 exchange.

articles.title: Us Construction’s Sophia Reyes: Inside the soaring demand for experiential retail and mixed-use projects
articles.narrative: Sophia Reyes, Director of Relationships at Us Construction, highlights the robust demand for experiential retail and mixed-use developments, particularly in South Florida, driven by an influx of residents seeking live-work-play environments. She notes that institutional investors are readily financing these larger mixed-use projects, while smaller, independent retailers and restaurants face significant challenges in securing funding due to rising construction costs. Reyes observes a post-COVID surge in experiential retail, with consumers prioritizing interactive experiences over traditional bar or club settings, leading to a resurgence in physical retail spaces.

articles.title: Houston’s Griffin Partners adds chief operating officer, chief portfolio officer
articles.narrative: Griffin Partners, a Houston-based commercial real estate investment and development firm, has promoted Kelly Agent to Chief Operating Officer and Lee Moreland to the newly created role of Chief Portfolio Officer. These executive appointments are intended to support the firm's continued growth across the Sunbelt and Mountain West regions. The expanded leadership structure aims to strengthen Griffin's vertically integrated investment platform, which encompasses acquisition, development, property and construction management, asset management, and disposition for its national portfolio of industrial, office, and mixed-use properties.

articles.title: Chicago’s Whitney Architects announces leadership transition
articles.narrative: Whitney Architects, a Chicago-based architecture and interior design firm, has announced a leadership transition as part of its long-term growth strategy. Founder Mark Stenftenagel will move to Chairman of the Board, with Rick Joutras appointed as Chief Executive Officer. Melissa Bogusch will continue in her role as President, as the firm focuses on strategic design for workplace, hospitality, life science, and experiential environments.

articles.title: Marcus & Millichap negotiates sale of Comfort Suites property in Johnson Creek
articles.narrative: Marcus & Millichap has brokered the sale of the 100-room Comfort Suites Johnson Creek Conference Center in Johnson Creek, Wisconsin. The property, built in 2005, is strategically located off Interstate 94, benefiting from its proximity to local attractions and a strong conference facility.

data_points.property_name: Comfort Suites Johnson Creek Conference Center
data_points.notable_features: Features an indoor pool, fitness and business centers, and a conference facility with meeting spaces accommodating over 500 guests. Located near Johnson Creek Premium Outlets and Milford Hills Hunt Club.

articles.title: Bain, 11North spend $300M on open-air retail portfolio
articles.narrative: A joint venture between Bain Capital Real Estate and 11North Partners has acquired a portfolio of five open-air retail centers across multiple states for approximately $300 million. The portfolio, totaling 757,000 square feet, boasts a strong tenant mix and 93 percent occupancy. This acquisition aligns with the partners' strategy of investing in grocery-anchored retail, which they view as offering compelling risk-adjusted fundamentals in the current real estate market.

data_points.notable_features: Portfolio includes properties in Carlsbad, CA; Falls Church, VA; Altamonte Springs, FL; and Sugar Land, TX.

articles.market_intelligence: Open-air, grocery-anchored retail is demonstrating compelling risk-adjusted fundamentals in the real estate landscape. Investors are gravitating to these assets as strong performers in the retail sector.

articles.title: HUD trims environmental review for federally-backed multifamily projects
articles.narrative: The Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) has issued an interim rule to streamline the approval process for federally-backed multifamily housing projects, particularly those involving affordable housing. This change eliminates a final environmental review sign-off for developments exceeding 200 units or with mortgages over $5 million, a step HUD deemed redundant and a cause of unnecessary delays. The move aligns with the Trump administration's broader deregulation agenda aimed at reducing federal permitting and environmental hurdles, a long-standing request from housing developers and industry groups who argue that layered reviews complicate financing timelines for affordable housing projects.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)

articles.title: PolicyPro: Development deals hit Council roadblock, new charter commission eyes housing rules
articles.narrative: The Monitor Point apartment complex, a proposed 1,150-unit development on the Greenpoint waterfront, faces a political stalemate in the City Council. Council member Lincoln Restler has rejected the project without a commitment to more income-restricted housing and the completion of Bushwick Inlet Park. The Gotham Organization, the developer, plans three residential towers with 40% of units (460 apartments) designated as permanently income-restricted for households earning 60% of the area median income, while the remaining 690 units would be market-rate. The developer has invested approximately $130 million in development rights, MTA facilities, and infrastructure, arguing that further affordability requirements are financially unfeasible. The project is now a candidate to test the city's new housing appeals board, highlighting the ongoing YIMBY-versus-NIMBY debate in the city.

data_points.notable_features: 460 units (40%) would be permanently income-restricted for households earning 60% of area median income.
data_points.project_notes: The project involves a lease of land from the Greenpoint Monitor Museum and the Metropolitan Transportation Authority. The developer has spent approximately $130 million on development rights, replacement MTA facilities, and infrastructure.

articles.title: Rotem Rosen nabs $135M in Israeli bond financing for NYC hotel, Miami site
articles.market: New York City, NY / Miami, FL
articles.narrative: MRR Development, led by Rotem Rosen, secured $135 million in bond financing from the Israeli market to refinance its Hotel Indigo property on New York City's Lower East Side and a development site in Miami. This financing, obtained at a 6.2 percent interest rate, replaces a previous $113 million bond issuance from 2022 that carried a lower 4.5 percent rate. The transaction highlights the continued, albeit more cautious, appeal of the Israeli bond market for U.S. real estate developers, particularly those with existing ties to the country.

data_points.address: 171 Ludlow Street, New York City, NY
data_points.project_notes: The Miami development site was acquired in 2022 and allows for up to 1.9 million SF of mixed-use development. The Hotel Indigo was purchased in 2018.

articles.financing: Israeli bond market financing at 6.2% interest rate; overall loan-to-value ratio of 55 percent.

articles.title: Gary Barnett’s next Midtown move?
articles.narrative: A pre-foreclosure suit has been filed against the owner of 110 East 55th Street, an office building in Midtown Manhattan, by a construction company that recently acquired the mortgage. This action is seen as a strategic move by Extell Development, led by Gary Barnett, to assemble a large development site on Park Avenue. The building is considered one of the final pieces needed for Extell's expansive project, which already includes a recently acquired development site at 405-417 Park Avenue and air rights from Central Synagogue.

data_points.address: 110 East 55th Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Adjacent to Extell's recently acquired development site at 405-417 Park Avenue.
data_points.project_notes: The pre-foreclosure suit was filed by an entity tied to JT Magen, which took over the mortgage from Flagstar Bank in March 2026. Extell Development is assembling a large development site in Midtown, with plans for up to 700,000 square feet of rentable office space.

articles.title: From adviser to adversary: A timeline of Floyd Mayweather’s relationship with Jona Rechnitz
articles.narrative: Floyd Mayweather Jr. has filed a lawsuit against his former investment manager, Jona Rechnitz, alleging fraud and the diversion of $175 million. The lawsuit claims Rechnitz misrepresented the terms of several real estate deals Mayweather pursued in New York City, Chicago, and Miami, including a purported $402 million acquisition of a 1,000-unit affordable rental portfolio in New York City and a $100 million investment in luxury Manhattan rentals. Mayweather alleges he only acquired a minority interest in the affordable housing portfolio and made a small, later liquidated, equity investment in the luxury rentals, contrary to Rechnitz's assurances. The lawsuit also claims Mayweather failed to close on a stake in the former Versace Mansion in Miami Beach and that Rechnitz diverted $15 million related to an investment in SL Green.

articles.title: The most talked about part of Mamdani’s housing plan wasn’t in his housing plan
articles.narrative: This article clarifies a misunderstanding regarding Mayor Zohran Mamdani's housing plan, specifically addressing a Wall Street Journal report about potential rent freeze exemptions for affordable housing landlords. The administration stated that the practice of restructuring rents on vacant units in affordable housing is not a new policy within the housing plan, but rather a longstanding tool used by the Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HPD) during loan modifications with affordable housing operators. This allows rents to be adjusted up to the level of a newly constructed affordable unit with similar income restrictions, but is used in minimal and rare situations.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Department of Housing Preservation and Development

articles.title: “Perpetual danger” for Fisher Island families: lawsuit filed over “secret” $400M fuel depot deal with Miami-Dade
articles.narrative: Fisher Island residents have filed a lawsuit against developer HRP, alleging a "secret" $400 million deal with Miami-Dade County for a fuel depot site. The residents claim HRP reneged on an earlier agreement to demolish the existing fuel bunker, remediate the land, and develop luxury condominiums, instead pursuing a sale of the entire 9.6-acre property to the county. The lawsuit seeks to compel HRP to honor its original commitment, which included conveying 4 acres to the Fisher Island Community Association for development. Residents express concerns about the safety of the aging fuel depot, citing its proximity to homes and a school, and its location in a hurricane flood zone.

data_points.project_notes: HRP previously acquired the 9.6-acre property for $180 million in October. The site contains a fuel depot with 28 million gallons of combustible fuel in century-old tanks. The county's interest in the site is to ensure continued fuel supply for cruise ships at PortMiami, with the current lease set to expire in 2027.

articles.title: Billionaire Jeff Greene’s latest West Palm Beach resi plans tap Live Local
articles.narrative: Billionaire developer Jeff Greene has proposed a 25-story, 366-unit apartment tower in West Palm Beach, marking his first project under Florida's Live Local Act. The development will incorporate a prefabricated mass timber system, making it one of the tallest of its kind in Florida, and will preserve two historic facades of a former fire station on the site. The project aims to address the demand for workforce housing, with 148 units (40%) designated as affordable, integrated throughout the building alongside market-rate units.

data_points.address: 120 South Dixie Highway, West Palm Beach, FL
data_points.notable_features: 25-story apartment tower, one of Florida's tallest mass timber buildings, 40% workforce housing units, preserves historic fire station facades.
data_points.project_notes: Project proposed under Florida's Live Local Act, which overrides some local zoning rules. Workforce housing units and market-rate units will be mixed throughout the building and share amenities.

articles.title: Ansin starts Miramar megaproject as Sunbeam pushes deeper into real estate
articles.narrative: Sunbeam Properties, in partnership with Stiles, has commenced construction on Miramar Cove, a significant mixed-use megadevelopment in Miramar, FL. The 125-acre project will feature nearly 3,000 residential units, substantial retail and office space, and a 185-key hotel. This development marks a deeper push into real estate for the Ansin family's Sunbeam, building on their long-standing presence in the region.

data_points.notable_features: Project centerpiece is a 5.5-acre water basin, with 5 miles of walking paths and over 30 acres of open park space.
data_points.project_notes: The 125-acre site was purchased approximately 70 years ago for about $100 per acre. Sunbeam proposed the project in 2024 and secured city and commission approvals.

articles.title: MIA pays $19M for Duty Free warehouse amid $14B airport expansion
articles.narrative: Miami International Airport (MIA) acquired a 68,000-square-foot industrial warehouse from Duty Free Americas for $19.2 million. The property, located at 1800 Northwest 70th Avenue, will initially serve as indoor storage and a construction staging site for MIA's extensive $14 billion modernization and expansion plan. Following the completion of the modernization work, the airport intends to reposition the asset for revenue-generating commercial uses. The transaction, which took nearly two years to finalize due to various hurdles including permitting and zoning, was brokered by WGS Realty.

data_points.notable_features: Acquired as part of Miami International Airport's $14 billion modernization and expansion plan.
data_points.project_notes: The acquisition was financed through future aviation revenue bonds under MIA's land acquisition subprogram.

articles.market_intelligence: Miami-Dade's industrial market saw 3.2 million square feet leased in Q1, with asking rents rising 1.5% year-over-year to $17.04 per square foot. Industrial developments completed during the quarter totaled 1.4 million square feet, increasing vacancy slightly to 7.1% from 6.9%. Another 2.9 million square feet of industrial space is currently under construction across South Florida. Industrial cap rates in South Florida averaged 6.3% in Q1.

articles.title: Chicago’s top construction permits: Week ending May 27, 2026
articles.narrative: This article details the top construction permits issued in Chicago for the week ending May 27, 2026. The largest permit, valued at $21 million, was granted to the Art Institute of Chicago for interior alterations to the Rice Building, creating the new Grainger Center for Conservation & Science. Other significant permits include a $13.8 million interior renovation for an airline lounge at O'Hare International Airport and a $7 million conversion of a bank into a medical care facility in Bronzeville. The week's activity saw a slight decrease in total value compared to the previous week.

articles.title: Jewelers Building bets on luxury amenities to keep office tenants coming
articles.narrative: CRG and Prime Group are investing $25 million into renovating the historic Jewelers Building in Downtown Chicago, adding luxury amenities such as a fine dining restaurant, coworking space, and rooftop bars. This renovation aims to attract and retain office tenants, building on recent leasing momentum including a significant expansion by Clayco and new headquarters for Sheridan Capital. The project reflects a strategic bet on high-end amenities to counter broader office market challenges, following the building's acquisition by CRG's parent company, Clayco, and Prime Group in summer 2024 after a foreclosure.

data_points.notable_features: Designated a Chicago landmark in 1994; rumored to have housed a speakeasy operated by Al Capone.
data_points.project_notes: Building was acquired in summer 2024 by Clayco and Prime Group after a year-long foreclosure proceeding, following a default on $51 million in debt by Canadian Dorchester Corporation in 2023.

articles.title: Distressed Hawthorne Race Course gets green light to subpoena Illinois officials
articles.narrative: Hawthorne Race Course, currently undergoing bankruptcy sale proceedings, has received federal court approval to subpoena Illinois state officials. The track's owners allege that the Illinois Department of Agriculture and its director improperly diverted $317,000 in state funds from Hawthorne while increasing funding to a direct competitor, Fairmount Park Casino & Racing. This legal action is part of the ongoing Chapter 11 bankruptcy case filed in February, which has seen the property listed for sale, including a reported $107 million offer last year. The track's financial distress has also been exacerbated by $6.7 million in contractor liens and a suspended harness racing license due to payroll violations.

data_points.project_notes: The property is currently in Chapter 11 bankruptcy proceedings. Plans to convert the racetrack into a 'racino' were hindered by $6.7 million in contractor liens for unpaid construction work. The track's harness racing license was suspended in January for payroll violations.

articles.title: Shai Wolkowicki hit with more lawsuits
articles.narrative: Chicago real estate investor Shai Wolkowicki is facing two new lawsuits, adding to his ongoing legal battles. One lawsuit, filed by Andrew Oshay, alleges financial mismanagement and diversion of funds intended for a South Side multifamily portfolio near the Obama Presidential Center, leading to property deterioration and default on senior debt. Separately, Old National Bank has initiated a $5.8 million foreclosure lawsuit against a venture involving Wolkowicki and his former partner Mitch Goltz, targeting vacant land parcels in Orland Park and Joliet that were once slated for medical office development. These new legal actions highlight significant financial distress across multiple real estate ventures associated with Wolkowicki.

data_points.project_notes: The land parcels were previously intended for medical office space development for Duly Health Care, which later backed out. The foreclosure is due to default on two loans that matured in February 2026.

articles.title: Chicago megadevelopment owners considering project merge
articles.narrative: Discussions are underway regarding a potential merger of two significant Chicago lakefront megaprojects: Bob Dunn's One Central and the Michael Reese hospital site development. The Michael Reese site, developed by Fairpoint Development as part of the GRIT consortium, faces a city deadline to revise its infrastructure plans. While the city is aware of the negotiations and Dunn's interest in expanding his lakefront holdings, the Michael Reese ownership group is reportedly resistant to partnering with Dunn.

data_points.original_plan: Michael Reese site initially slated for 2024 completion; One Central proposed in 2019 with a $20 billion vision and a $6.5 billion state subsidy for a transit hub, which was later spiked.
data_points.notable_features: Both projects are large-scale mixed-use developments located on the Chicago lakefront.
data_points.project_notes: The Michael Reese site was originally planned as a $3.8 billion mixed-use campus including the ARC Innovation Center, a medical research development, but that deal fell apart. One Central's transit hub plans were spiked in recent years, leaving the development in limbo.

articles.title: ICE planning to add more San Antonio office space as workforce dramatically increases
articles.narrative: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) is seeking an additional 65,000 square feet of office space in San Antonio, within the Loop 1604 boundaries, to accommodate a significant workforce increase. This expansion comes as the agency's budget has grown by nearly $80 million, enabling a planned 120 percent increase in personnel. The agency is also converting a recently acquired 640,000-square-foot warehouse into a detention center, a move that has faced strong opposition from local advocacy groups and city leaders.

data_points.project_notes: ICE is seeking an additional 65,000 square feet of office space within the Loop 1604 boundaries. The agency previously purchased a 640,000-square-foot warehouse for conversion into a detention center, expected to be operational by the end of 2026.

articles.market_intelligence: ICE's budget has increased by nearly $80 million, supporting a planned 120 percent workforce increase. Studies suggest property values decline near federal prisons.

articles.title: Airport-adjacent tract snapped up near Sherman
articles.narrative: A 209-acre agricultural tract near the North Texas Regional Airport in Sherman, TX, was sold to OTW Preston 209 Limited, an entity linked to Jim Hazard of Cresa. The land, previously listed for $9.4 million, was acquired from individual investor Sreeramulu Bokkisham. This transaction highlights the increasing investment in the Sherman-Denison area, driven by the airport's expansion plans and significant industrial developments like Texas Instruments' semiconductor plant.

data_points.notable_features: Located across from North Texas Regional Airport, listed for $45,000 per acre.
data_points.project_notes: The seller, Sreeramulu Bokkisham, acquired the land in 2022.

articles.market_intelligence: The North Texas Regional Airport is preparing to update its lighting and install a new taxiway to meet spillover demand from Dallas-area air travel. McKinney National Airport plans to complete a commercial terminal with passenger service by the end of 2026. Texas Instruments is developing a $40 billion semiconductor manufacturing plant on a 1,200-acre site in Sherman. Apple is also planning a laser equipment manufacturing plant in Sherman. Craig International is developing Preston Harbor, a 3,100-acre project in Denison.

articles.title: Affinius-led investor group finalizes nearly $4B Veris Residential takeover
articles.narrative: An investor group led by Affinius Capital and Vista Hill Partners has finalized its $3.5 billion acquisition of Veris Residential, a real estate investment trust. The all-cash transaction, which includes debt, was priced at $19 per share and marks the culmination of Veris Residential's five-year strategic shift from a diversified office and apartment portfolio to a solely residential enterprise. The acquisition includes a portfolio of Class A multifamily properties located across the New York City metro area, Boston, and New Jersey.

data_points.notable_features: Portfolio consists of Class A multifamily units.
data_points.project_notes: The investor buyout was priced at $19 per share of Veris common stock. Veris Residential was formerly known as Mack-Cali Realty Corp and had been divesting suburban office properties over the last five years to focus on residential assets.

articles.financing: The transaction included a roughly $2 billion bridge loan.

articles.title: The die is cast: Tilman Fertitta to buy Caesars Entertainment in $17B-plus deal
articles.narrative: Tilman Fertitta's Fertitta Entertainment has agreed to acquire Caesars Entertainment in an all-cash deal valued at approximately $17.6 billion, including the assumption of $12 billion in debt. This acquisition, which includes a "go-shop" period for Caesars to consider other offers until July 11, would expand Fertitta Entertainment's domestic casino resort portfolio to 60 properties. Fertitta has pursued Caesars for nearly a decade, previously proposing a merger with his Golden Nugget casino chain.

articles.title: Distress wave hits S2 Capital with $250M in CMBS loans flagged for special servicing
articles.narrative: Over $250 million in CMBS loans tied to three multifamily properties owned by S2 Capital have been transferred to special servicing due to underperformance. The properties, located in Grand Prairie, TX, Houston, TX, and Glendale, AZ, failed to maintain net cash flow at the levels required by their underwriting. This financial distress follows a recent capital call by S2 Capital, which aimed to raise $70 million in preferred equity but secured only $30 million, leading to warnings of a full loss of capital for equity investors in the REIT.

articles.title: Distressed San Jose resi tower sells for $175M, shifts to mixed-income
articles.narrative: A business entity led by developers Andrew Jacobson and Gary Dillabough, with involvement from Flow, has acquired The Fay residential tower in downtown San Jose for $175 million. The 23-story, 336-unit property, which had previously fallen into default, will be rebranded and renovated, with a focus on activating ground-floor commercial spaces. The acquisition marks Flow's entry into the West Coast market. Notably, the City of San Jose has leased 197 units within the building to provide affordable housing for middle-income households, including public servants, with subsidies for residents earning 80% to 110% of the area median income.

data_points.address: 10 East Reed Street, San Jose, CA
data_points.notable_features: 23-story building with 197 units designated as affordable housing for middle-income households.
data_points.project_notes: The property was previously in default. The City of San Jose leased 197 units for 10 years with a five-year extension option to provide affordable housing.

companies_people.label: BUYER
companies_people.firm_name: Business entity led by Andrew Jacobson and Gary Dillabough

articles.title: Academy of Art sells another church building — this time to an actual church
articles.narrative: Epic Church San Francisco is under contract to acquire a historic church building at 491 Post Street in Union Square from the Academy of Art University. The property, listed for $15 million, features a 1,000-seat sanctuary and 40-foot ceilings. This acquisition marks the church's fifth location since its founding and will nearly double its current footprint, allowing for expanded ministries and community engagement.

data_points.notable_features: Historic building designed by Reid Brothers, known for landmarks like the Fairmont Hotel; features 40-foot ceilings and a 1,000-seat sanctuary.
data_points.project_notes: The Academy of Art University previously used the building as a student auditorium after purchasing it in 2001. The seller is undertaking a citywide real estate selloff valued at $130 million.

articles.title: San Jose La Quinta latest Bay Area hotels in default
articles.narrative: The La Quinta Inn & Suites in San Jose has defaulted on a $16 million construction loan provided by Central Valley Community Bank. The 59-room hotel, completed in 2023, now faces potential foreclosure. This default is part of a broader trend of distress in the Bay Area's lodging sector, with numerous hotels experiencing loan defaults and foreclosures since 2023.

data_points.address: 1036 North Fourth Street, San Jose, CA
data_points.project_notes: Construction loan originated in 2019.

articles.title: CRE Capital Flows Unevenly in a K-Shaped Economy
articles.narrative: This article analyzes the persistent K-shaped economic recovery, five years after its emergence during the COVID-19 recession, and its uneven impact on commercial real estate capital flows. The author argues that while capital is available, it is highly selective, favoring strong sponsors and high-quality assets in top-performing markets. This divergence is evident across sectors, with luxury retail and top-tier multifamily performing well, while lower- and middle-market retail and renter-by-necessity multifamily struggle with oversupply and weaker fundamentals. Lenders are prioritizing assets with clear business plans and strong sponsorship, leading to creative deal structures that price risk accordingly.

people.name: Ryan Severino
people.title: Chief Economist and Head of Research

articles.title: How Supply Chain Volatility Will Impact Industrial Real Estate
articles.narrative: This article argues that ongoing supply chain volatility, driven by structural factors like geopolitical realignment, energy transition, labor constraints, and higher capital costs, is fundamentally reshaping the industrial real estate sector. The author contends that companies can no longer rely on temporary fixes for supply chain disruptions and are instead pursuing long-term network redesigns for greater resilience and operational flexibility. This shift necessitates that industrial owners and developers adapt by underwriting operations rather than just market comps, prioritizing supply chain-critical locations, and building flexibility into assets to accommodate evolving occupier needs such as increased trailer storage, automation, and light manufacturing capabilities.

people.name: Michael Carson
people.title: Head of Supply Chain & Logistics Advisory, APAC & EMEA

articles.title: Recovering Value from Underutilized Campus Space
articles.narrative: JLL's inaugural 2025 Higher Education Portfolio Benchmarking Survey highlights that universities and colleges are struggling with underperforming physical campuses, averaging $4.9 billion in value per institution, due to a significant gap between planned and actual classroom utilization. The survey found that 81% of institutions lack key performance indicators for space management, and only 50% track occupancy data, leading to anecdotal rather than evidence-based space decisions. JLL analysts suggest that systematic space management could generate annual cost savings of $2 million to $4 million, emphasizing that higher education treats its largest capital investment differently from corporations, which view space as a measurable, optimizable asset. The report proposes a phased solution involving sensor deployment, KPI establishment, and data integration into decision-making, asserting that optimizing real estate assets is the largest untapped funding source for institutional excellence.

articles.title: Senior Living Facilities: Don’t Forget the Outdoor Space
articles.narrative: This article, authored by landscape architect David McCullough, argues that outdoor spaces in senior living facilities are as crucial as indoor amenities for residents' well-being and should be prioritized from the outset of design. McCullough contends that modern seniors are more active and healthier than previous generations, requiring outdoor environments that support activities like pickleball, cooking, and socialization. He emphasizes that while capital investments vary by care level and location, strategically planned outdoor spaces can offer a decent return on investment and significantly improve residents' physical and mental health, reducing isolation and stress.

articles.title: Law Firm Taft Will Occupy Full Floor in Durst Midtown Tower
articles.narrative: National law firm Taft has signed a lease for a full floor at 1155 Avenue of the Americas, a Midtown tower owned by The Durst Organization, to establish its New York City office. The firm plans to significantly expand its presence in the city over the next five years, aiming for over 100 lawyers. This move marks a strategic expansion for Taft, allowing it to better serve clients nationwide by filling a key geographic gap in its operations.

data_points.address: 1155 Avenue of the Americas, New York City, NY

articles.title: Data Center Land Deals Surged 141% In Q1, Warping The Market For Everyone Else
articles.narrative: Sales of land designated for data center development surged by 141% in Q1 2026 compared to the previous year, reaching approximately $3.3 billion nationwide. This dramatic increase, which accounted for 30% of all development site spending, is driven by the dwindling availability of land with immediate access to power and favorable zoning. The scarcity of powered land has significantly inflated prices, making it challenging for other asset classes like industrial to compete, particularly in markets like Northern Virginia where industrial development has shifted due to data center land pricing. Experts anticipate this trend will continue as demand for computing power grows, but the sector faces a critical challenge in securing sufficient electricity capacity from energy providers.
articles.market_intelligence: Data center land deals accounted for 30% of all development site spending in Q1 2026, up from 19% in 2025. The number of data center sites sold decreased from 20 to 16, indicating a rise in value per site. Overall development site sales volume increased 55% year-over-year to $11.1 billion in Q1. In Q4 2025, developers added only 25 gigawatts of electricity capacity to their pipeline, half of the previous quarter. U.S. data center power demand is projected to increase from 31 GW in 2025 to 66 GW in 2027. Industrial development sales volume increased 65% year-over-year to $2.3 billion, with the number of sites sold rising only 19%. Multifamily development sales volume increased from $2 billion to $2.6 billion, and mixed-use site sales climbed from $1.2 billion to $2.4 billion. Private buyers accounted for 70% of Q1 development transactions.

articles.title: Downtown Atlanta Is Poised For Revival. Businesses Are Still Leaving
articles.narrative: Downtown Atlanta is undergoing a significant revitalization driven by billions in new development, including CIM Group's Centennial Yards, CP Group's rebranded CNN Center, and Atlanta Ventures' South Downtown mixed-use project. These initiatives aim to attract visitors and consumers, but the office market continues to struggle with a net loss of 2 million square feet since 2020. The exodus of "legacy tenants" to submarkets like Midtown is attributed to aging office stock, landlord struggles, and perceptions of crime and vagrancy. However, some experts believe the office ecosystem is transforming, with a potential for new "urban pioneer" tenants and government entities drawn by affordable rents, though the lack of dense residential population remains a critical challenge for sustained growth.

articles.title: With Rent Control Looming, Investors Say It's Hard To Bet On Boston
articles.narrative: Real estate executives in Massachusetts are expressing significant concern over the potential impact of a statewide rent control ballot measure, which is creating regulatory uncertainty and making long-term investment planning difficult for investors in Boston and across the state. The proposed measure, capping rent increases at 5% or the consumer price index (whichever is lower), is seen by many as a major risk that could fundamentally alter the multifamily market. Investors are already reportedly diverting capital elsewhere, with some institutional buyers halting investments in Massachusetts due to the chilling effect of the proposed policy, compounded by negative macroeconomic factors like high construction costs and stagnant population growth. While some argue the impact might be limited to markets with high rent growth, the prevailing sentiment among Boston-based real estate professionals is that rent control would stifle new housing supply, which is already slowing due to high interest rates and construction costs.

companies_people.label: OTHER
companies_people.firm_name: Tufts University Center for State Policy Analysis

articles.market_intelligence: Boston has experienced a sharp drop in new multifamily projects constructed, with deliveries decreasing from 2,765 units in Q1 2025 to 1,736 units last quarter. Year-over-year rent growth in Boston's first quarter flattened to 0.3%, marking a nearly five-year low.

articles.title: Jameis Winston, Mark Ingram Among Investors In Modular Building Firm
articles.narrative: Kinexx Modular Construction, a Chicago-based modular home company, is raising capital through a crowdfunding campaign to expand its operations into eight new urban markets across the country. The company specializes in building on challenging urban infill lots that traditional construction firms often avoid. The expansion aims to address the significant number of vacant lots in cities like Detroit, Baltimore, and St. Louis, leveraging modular construction as a solution for affordable housing development.

articles.title: Mori Building Buys Stake In SL Green's Next Trophy As Japan's U.S. CRE Interest Deepens
articles.narrative: Mori Building Co., a Tokyo-based developer, has acquired a 49% equity stake in SL Green's planned trophy office tower at 346 Madison Avenue in Midtown Manhattan. The deal values the asset at $175 million and marks Mori Building Co.'s first development project in New York City, with SL Green serving as development and leasing manager. This partnership highlights the increasing interest of Japanese firms in U.S. real estate, particularly in high-quality office assets, as they seek diversification and higher returns amidst a slowing domestic economy.

data_points.address: 346 Madison Ave, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: KPF-designed 46-story tower with a landscaped terrace, a two-floor amenity space including a 215-seat auditorium, and a tenant-only padel court.
data_points.project_notes: SL Green previously acquired 346 Madison Avenue and an adjacent site in 2025 for $160 million.

people.name: Jacob Feingold
people.title: Managing Director and Head of Originations

articles.market_intelligence: Japanese investors were the second-biggest international investors in U.S. real estate in 2025, behind Canada. Trophy office owners in Manhattan were able to charge three figures per square foot in rents in Q1. Japanese firms are diversifying into other geographies and asset classes due to a slowing domestic economy and rising cost of borrowing.
